2.4.a.info:
EtherChannel (also called Link Aggregation in the IEEE world, or simply a port-channel on Cisco IOS) is the technology that bundles 2 to 8 parallel physical Ethernet links between two devices into a single logical interface. Once bundled, the switch, STP, and upper-layer protocols all see one interface named Port-channel N (for example, Port-channel 1 or Po1). The individual physical ports become invisible to the rest of the system — they are now just member links of the logical channel.

Three problems it solves at once: (1) Bandwidth aggregation — four 1 Gbps links bundled together give you 4 Gbps of aggregate throughput between the two switches. (2) Redundancy — if one physical cable or port fails, the channel stays up on the remaining links; you only lose that link's share of bandwidth, not connectivity. (3) STP optimization — this is the killer feature. STP treats the entire port-channel as one link, so it will NEVER block individual member links. Without EtherChannel, STP would block three of your four cables and you'd get 1 Gbps of usable bandwidth despite having 4 Gbps of physical capacity.

Where you see EtherChannel in the real world: switch-to-switch uplinks in access/distribution/core designs, server-to-switch NIC teaming (the server side calls it LAG or bond), and router-to-switch inter-site links. The CCNA exam focuses on switch-to-switch EtherChannels configured on Cisco IOS.

What makes EtherChannel different from just 'plugging in more cables': normal STP behavior would block redundant paths to prevent loops. EtherChannel works BECAUSE STP is told 'this is one link, not four' — loop prevention is handled internally by the EtherChannel load-balancing hash, which guarantees frames from the same flow always take the same member link (no reordering, no duplication).

hack.memory: EtherChannel = 'bundle of cables wearing one nametag.' 4 physical cables, 1 logical interface (Po1). STP sees the nametag, not the cables, so it cannot block any individual member. 2-8 links per bundle. Think 'tennis doubles' — two players, one team name on the scoreboard.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, connect Switch A and Switch B with 4 cables (Gi0/1-4). Before EtherChannel, run 'show spanning-tree' — you'll see 3 of the 4 ports in BLK state. Now configure 'interface range Gi0/1 - 4', 'channel-group 1 mode active' on both switches. Run 'show etherchannel summary' — all four ports show (P) under Po1. Run 'show spanning-tree' again — only Po1 appears, and it's FWD. This before/after comparison is the single clearest demo of why EtherChannel exists.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 opens with exactly this 'STP blocks your extra cables' demo — watch that segment. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10 frames EtherChannel as the answer to STP's bandwidth waste. The exam tests the concept ('what does EtherChannel do?') and the terminology (Port-channel = logical, member ports = physical).

2.4.a.info:
This is the single most important behavioral rule of EtherChannel: STP treats a port-channel as ONE link. STP runs its usual algorithm (elect root bridge, calculate root path cost, block redundant paths), but during that calculation the port-channel counts as a single interface — not as multiple separate links. This means none of the member ports are ever blocked individually; either the whole port-channel is forwarding, or the whole port-channel is blocking.

Why this matters: without EtherChannel, four parallel cables between two switches look like four separate links to STP. STP sees the loop and blocks three of them to leave one forwarding path. Result: only 1 Gbps of usable bandwidth out of 4 Gbps of physical capacity. With EtherChannel, STP sees one logical interface. There's no loop to prevent at the STP layer (the EtherChannel load balancer prevents the loop internally by hashing each flow to exactly one member link). All four cables forward simultaneously, and you get the full 4 Gbps.

STP path cost on a port-channel: the port-channel's STP cost is based on the total aggregated bandwidth, not an individual member's speed. Four 1 Gbps members = 4 Gbps aggregate = STP cost of 3 (using the default long-form path costs). This influences root path cost calculations — aggregated links are naturally preferred over single links of the same speed.

Failure behavior: if a member link fails, the port-channel stays up (with reduced bandwidth), the STP topology does NOT reconverge, and traffic keeps flowing. This is a huge advantage over non-bundled links, where a failure would trigger STP reconvergence (up to 30-50 seconds of disruption with classic STP, or 1-2 seconds with RSTP). Only when ALL members of a channel fail does STP see the port-channel go down and start reconverging.

right.label: With EtherChannel
items: Single cable failure = no STP event

hack.memory: STP sees Po1, not the cables underneath. 'One name, one decision.' STP cannot block a member port because it doesn't see members — it only sees the channel. Bonus: aggregated bandwidth = lower STP cost = the bundled path tends to win root-path-cost calculations anyway.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, connect two switches with 3 cables (no channel yet). Run 'show spanning-tree' — two of the three ports are BLK. Note the root path cost on the non-root switch. Now bundle them with 'channel-group 1 mode active' both sides. Run 'show spanning-tree' again — only Po1 shows, it's FWD, and the root path cost is LOWER than before (because aggregated BW = lower cost). Finally, shut down one member link. Note STP does not reconverge — Po1 stays FWD with reduced bandwidth.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 (EtherChannel) explicitly demonstrates this 'STP sees one link' rule. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10 and the Spanning Tree chapters (Chapter 9) reinforce it. Exam-style question: 'when a member link in a Layer 2 EtherChannel fails, what happens to STP?' Answer: nothing — the port-channel stays up, no reconvergence, no topology change.

2.4.a.info:
For an EtherChannel to form and stay healthy, every member port on a switch must have identical configuration. Any mismatch on any single setting causes that port to be suspended from the channel (shown as the 's' flag in show etherchannel summary). A suspended port is physically up but not forwarding any traffic — it's frozen out of the bundle until you fix the mismatch.

The five settings that must match across all members: (1) Speed — all ports must negotiate or be hard-set to the same speed. You cannot mix a 1 Gbps link with a 10 Gbps link in the same channel. (2) Duplex — all full-duplex (the modern default for anything other than shared-media hubs, which you won't see on the CCNA). (3) VLAN / access VLAN — if the channel is an access port, all members must be in the same access VLAN. (4) Trunk configuration — if the channel is a trunk, all members need identical trunk mode, encapsulation (dot1q), allowed VLAN list, and native VLAN. (5) STP settings — same port priority, same port cost, and ideally the same BPDU filter/guard settings.

Why mismatches matter beyond 'it won't work': if a mismatch went undetected and both mismatched ports forwarded, you would have an STP loop (because the mismatched port would no longer be part of the logical port-channel STP sees). Suspension is EtherChannel's safety mechanism — rather than letting a dangerous misconfiguration forward traffic, the switch isolates the offending port.

Best practice to avoid mismatches: configure everything on the port-channel interface, not on the individual member interfaces. The port-channel automatically pushes its config down to its members. So the workflow becomes: create the port-channel with channel-group N mode [mode] on the range of physical ports, then switch to interface Port-channel N and configure trunking, VLANs, STP, etc. there. IOS keeps the members synchronized.

hack.memory: Member port matching = 'uniform dress code.' Any player out of uniform gets benched — that's the 's' (suspended) flag. The five must-match items: Speed, Duplex, VLAN, Trunk config, STP. Mnemonic: S-D-V-T-S, 'Soldiers Don't Violate The Standard.' Fix the mismatch and the port auto-rejoins — no shut/no-shut required.
hack.practice: Build a 4-port EtherChannel in Packet Tracer and verify 'show etherchannel summary' shows all members (P). Now break it in three ways, one at a time: (1) change one member's access VLAN, (2) change one member's speed, (3) change one member's trunk allowed-VLAN list. Each time, run the summary and confirm the offending port now shows (s). Fix the mismatch and watch it bounce back to (P) without any reload. This cements the troubleshooting muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 and OCG Chapter 10 both list the matching requirements. The exam loves this: you'll see 'show etherchannel summary' output with one port in (s) and be asked why. Answer is always 'config mismatch on that port.' Best-practice answer: configure on the port-channel interface, not the members.

2.4.a.info:
EtherChannel's load balancing decides which physical member link carries each frame. The rule to burn in: load balancing is per-flow, never per-packet. All frames belonging to the same conversation (same source/destination pair, depending on the hash method) are pinned to the same physical link. This is critical because per-packet load balancing would cause out-of-order delivery, which TCP hates.

How the hash works: the switch computes a hash of selected header fields (source MAC, destination MAC, source IP, destination IP, or combinations) and uses the result to pick one member link. Same input fields every time = same member link every time, so the flow never gets re-ordered.

Common hash methods (configured globally with port-channel load-balance [method]):

  • src-mac — hash on source MAC only. Works for simple L2 scenarios but all traffic from one host uses one member.
  • dst-mac — hash on destination MAC only. Good when many hosts talk to one server.
  • src-dst-mac — hash on both MACs. Better distribution.
  • src-ip / dst-ip — L3 equivalents of the MAC variants.
  • src-dst-ip — the recommended default for L3 and mixed traffic. Produces the best distribution across members in most real-world networks.

Why it matters: a poorly chosen hash method can leave you with 'elephant flows' where one giant TCP conversation consumes one entire member link while the others sit idle. Picking a hash that matches your traffic pattern (for example, src-dst-ip when many clients talk to many servers) spreads traffic more evenly.

Verification: show etherchannel load-balance shows the currently configured hash method. To see actual per-link utilization, use show interfaces Gi0/1 | include rate on each member — if one member is at 90% and the rest are at 5%, your hash isn't distributing well for your traffic mix.

hack.memory: Load balancing = 'which cashier line does this customer stand in?' Hash decides, and a customer (flow) always gets the same cashier (link). Never split a single customer across multiple lines — that's per-packet, and TCP freaks out. Best default = src-dst-ip (both ends of the conversation influence the choice).
hack.practice: In a Packet Tracer lab with a 2-member EtherChannel, run 'show etherchannel load-balance' — note the default method. Change it globally with 'port-channel load-balance src-dst-ip' and verify. Generate a ping from one PC through the channel — all packets from that ping take ONE member link (you can verify by shutting down the 'chosen' member and watching the ping fail briefly before re-hashing to the other member).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 touches load balancing. OCG Chapter 10 covers it more deeply. The exam is light on load-balancing methods but heavy on the per-flow concept — expect a 'true/false: EtherChannel balances load per-packet' type question. Answer: false, per-flow.

2.4.a.info:
EtherChannel can be built using one of three negotiation approaches. Knowing all three and their trade-offs is a guaranteed exam topic.

(1) LACP — Link Aggregation Control Protocol. Defined in IEEE 802.3ad (later moved to IEEE 802.1AX). Open standard. Works between any vendor's devices. Uses two modes: active (initiates) and passive (responds only). Requires at least one side to be active. This is the recommended protocol for nearly all modern deployments.

(2) PAgP — Port Aggregation Protocol. Cisco proprietary. Works only between Cisco devices. Uses two modes: desirable (initiates, equivalent to LACP active) and auto (responds only, equivalent to LACP passive). Same 'at least one initiator' rule. Legacy protocol — still works but offers no advantage over LACP in modern Cisco-only environments.

(3) Static / mode on. No negotiation protocol at all. Both sides are configured as mode on and the channel forms based purely on local config. No negotiation packets are exchanged, and therefore no error detection: if you misconfigure one side, the other side won't notice, and you may create L2 loops. Cannot be mixed with LACP or PAgP modes — only on + on forms a static channel.

Which one to choose on the exam and in real life: LACP, LACP, LACP. Use PAgP only when maintaining an existing PAgP configuration. Use mode on almost never — only for equipment that genuinely doesn't support LACP or PAgP. The exam tests all three but expects you to recommend LACP for new deployments.

Protocol mixing rules: both sides of a link must use the same protocol family. LACP modes cannot pair with PAgP modes. LACP/PAgP modes cannot pair with mode on. If you try to mix them, the channel will not form — the ports will either stay individual (I) or bounce.

hack.memory: Three protocols, three personalities: LACP = 'the diplomat' (standards-based, talks to anyone). PAgP = 'the Cisco insider' (Cisco-only, same concept). Static on = 'the cowboy' (no talking, no safety net, don't do it). Default answer on exam and in real life: LACP active on both sides.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, build the same 2-port EtherChannel three different ways: (1) LACP — 'channel-group 1 mode active' both sides. (2) PAgP — 'channel-group 1 mode desirable' both sides. (3) Static — 'channel-group 1 mode on' both sides. Verify each with 'show etherchannel summary'. Note that the protocol column shows LACP, PAgP, or '-' (none) respectively. Then try to mix them (LACP on one side, PAgP on the other) — observe the channel does NOT form.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 walks all three protocols. OCG Chapter 10 presents them in a comparison table. Classic exam setup: 'which EtherChannel protocol is an IEEE open standard?' (LACP) 'which is Cisco proprietary?' (PAgP) 'which does not use negotiation?' (static/mode on). Know the three names cold.

2.4.a.info:
When you issue channel-group 1 mode active on a physical interface, IOS automatically creates a logical interface called Port-channel 1 (abbreviated Po1). This logical interface is where you should configure all Layer 2 and Layer 3 settings for the channel — trunk mode, allowed VLANs, access VLAN, IP address (for L3 channels), STP settings, and so on.

Why not configure on members? Because the port-channel pushes its configuration down to every member port automatically. If you configure on the port-channel, IOS keeps all members in sync. If you configure on individual members and they drift apart, you'll get suspension (the 's' flag). Doing it on the port-channel eliminates that entire class of mistakes.

Typical L2 trunk configuration:

interface range Gi0/1 - 4
 channel-group 1 mode active
!
interface Port-channel 1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
 switchport trunk native vlan 99

After this, run show running-config interface Gi0/1 and you'll see the trunk configuration showing up on the physical port too — IOS copied it from Po1 down to the members. That's the port-channel doing its job.

Typical L3 configuration: same idea but with no switchport on the members first, then ip address on the Port-channel interface (never on the members themselves).

Creation and deletion: you do NOT manually run interface Port-channel 1 before creating the channel — IOS creates it for you the first time a physical port is added with channel-group 1. If you remove all members (no channel-group 1 on each), the Port-channel interface stays in the config as an empty shell; remove it with no interface Port-channel 1.

layers.label: switchport mode trunk + allowed VLANs + native VLAN
Auto-pushed down to members Gi0/1, Gi0/2, Gi0/3, Gi0/4

hack.memory: Port-channel interface = 'the HR manager for the team.' You hand the manager one set of policies, and the manager distributes them to every team member. Configure trunk, VLAN, STP, IP on Po1 — all four members automatically inherit. Configure on a member directly = you've gone around HR = mismatch risk.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, build an LACP EtherChannel. Before configuring any trunking, run 'show run interface Gi0/1' and 'show run interface Port-channel 1' — both empty. Now add 'switchport mode trunk' and 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30' on interface Port-channel 1. Run 'show run interface Gi0/1' again — the trunk config is now there too. That's the auto-push from Po1 → members. Never configure trunking on a member directly during normal operation.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 emphasizes configuring on the port-channel interface. OCG Chapter 10 states this as best practice. The exam asks: 'where do you configure VLAN/trunk settings for an EtherChannel?' Answer: on the Port-channel interface. Also know: the Port-channel interface is auto-created when you add the first member with channel-group.

2.4.b.info:
LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol) is the open-standard EtherChannel negotiation protocol, originally defined in IEEE 802.3ad and later moved to IEEE 802.1AX when the IEEE reorganized aggregation standards. It is the recommended protocol for nearly all EtherChannel deployments and the primary protocol tested on the CCNA exam.

Why LACP wins over PAgP: it works between any vendor's devices — Cisco-to-Arista, Cisco-to-Juniper, Cisco-to-Linux-server bond, Cisco-to-Dell — because it's a standard. PAgP only works Cisco-to-Cisco, which is a dead end in any multi-vendor environment. Since nearly every enterprise has some non-Cisco gear somewhere, LACP is the safer default even in all-Cisco environments.

How LACP works: each side sends LACPDUs (LACP Data Units) out every member port. Default timer is 30 seconds slow mode (or 1 second fast mode with lacp rate fast, but CCNA uses slow mode). The LACPDUs carry the local system's ID, port priority, and key information. Each side uses the received LACPDUs to verify the partner is reachable and to elect the active/standby members when more than 8 ports are configured.

LACPDU loss detection: if a member port stops receiving LACPDUs for 3 consecutive intervals (90 seconds in slow mode), LACP declares that member failed and removes it from the channel. This is how LACP detects partner-side failures that don't manifest as physical link-down events (for example, a software crash on the partner that leaves the physical link up but the partner no longer forwarding).

Configuration: channel-group N mode active or channel-group N mode passive on each member interface. The N is the channel group number and is locally significant — it can be different on each switch; only the physical wiring matters.

hack.memory: LACP = 'Let's All Chat Politely' — the diplomat. IEEE 802.3ad. Open standard. Multi-vendor. Sends LACPDUs every 30s (slow mode). Loses LACPDUs for 90s straight = member declared dead. Burn the standard number in your head: 802.3ad (also 802.1AX in modern documents).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure 'channel-group 1 mode active' both sides and run 'show lacp neighbor' — you'll see the partner's system ID, port priority, and flags. Also try 'show lacp counters' to watch LACPDU packets increment every 30 seconds. Shut down one member — 'show lacp neighbor' loses that entry. Bring it back — it returns within ~30s when the next LACPDU arrives.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 covers LACP in depth. OCG Chapter 10 presents LACP as the default recommendation. Exam-guaranteed question: 'what IEEE standard defines LACP?' → 802.3ad (or 802.1AX in newer OCG editions — both are correct, 802.3ad is the most common answer).

2.4.b.info:
LACP active mode configures a port to actively initiate LACP negotiation. The port starts transmitting LACPDUs as soon as it comes up, without waiting for the partner to speak first. Command: channel-group N mode active.

Why active is the default recommendation: if both sides are active, they both initiate, and the channel forms in the minimum time. There's no 'who goes first' problem because both are shouting hello at the same time. If one side is active and the other is passive, the active side's LACPDUs trigger the passive side to respond, and the channel forms. Either way, at least one active side guarantees success.

Rule to memorize: for a channel to form, at least ONE side must be in active mode (or in the PAgP equivalent, desirable mode, but we're focused on LACP here). If both sides are passive, neither will initiate and the channel will never form — both ports will sit in standalone (I) state forever.

Common real-world default: many engineers set both sides to active because it's the most robust and has no downside. The only reason to use passive is when you want to avoid sending LACPDUs until the partner is confirmed (for example, on a port that might be cabled to a non-LACP device where unsolicited LACPDUs could cause log spam).

Verification: show etherchannel summary shows 'LACP' as the protocol and each member as (P) when bundled. show lacp neighbor shows the partner's port, system ID, and flags (A = active, P = passive in the flags column).

hack.memory: Active = 'I'll say hi first.' The extrovert of LACP modes. Both-active is the safest default. Rule: 'at least one active per link.' Command to muscle-memory: 'channel-group 1 mode active'. If you forget everything else about EtherChannel config, remember this one line — it works 95% of the time.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure both sides with 'channel-group 1 mode active' and verify channel forms within ~30s. Then unconfigure one side ('no channel-group 1 mode active' on each member, back to no EtherChannel) and reconfigure that side as 'mode passive'. Re-verify — channel still forms because the other side is active. Run 'show lacp neighbor' and note the flag column: 'A' = active, 'P' = passive.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 teaches 'mode active' as the go-to. OCG Chapter 10 agrees. The exam tests the command syntax and the concept of 'at least one active'. Memorize: 'channel-group N mode active' produces an LACP channel where this side initiates.

2.4.b.info:
LACP passive mode configures a port to wait for LACPDUs from the partner before speaking. A passive port does not initiate negotiation — it only responds. Command: channel-group N mode passive.

What happens with a passive port: the physical link comes up, but the port sits quiet, waiting for an LACPDU to arrive. When one arrives (because the other side is configured as active), the passive port starts sending its own LACPDUs in response. The channel then forms through normal LACP negotiation. If no LACPDU ever arrives (because the other side is also passive, or has no LACP configuration), the passive port remains standalone (I) forever.

The dangerous combination: passive + passive = NO channel. Both sides sit waiting for the other to speak first. This is the classic exam trap and a real-world misconfiguration that leaves engineers scratching their heads. Always check: does at least one side have mode active?

When to use passive: rarely. One legitimate scenario is a port connected to a device where LACP might not be configured — using passive prevents you from flooding the partner with unsolicited LACPDUs. But even then, 'mode active + unidirectional LACP detection' is usually a better choice. Most textbook and real-world deployments use active on both sides.

Verification: show lacp neighbor on a port configured as passive whose partner is active will show the partner's flags as 'A' (active). Your own side's flags are 'P' (passive).

hack.memory: Passive = 'I'll only respond if spoken to.' The wallflower. Needs the other side to be active. Passive + Passive = two wallflowers at a dance = no channel ever. The trap: engineer configures both switches identically as 'passive' and wonders why the channel doesn't form. Always check: at least one side active.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure Switch A as 'mode passive' and Switch B as 'mode passive'. Verify 'show etherchannel summary' shows both ports as (I) standalone (or blank, depending on IOS) — channel does NOT form. Now change Switch B to 'mode active' — within 30 seconds the channel forms and both members show (P). This cements the 'at least one active' rule experientially.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 explicitly labs passive+passive to demonstrate the failure. OCG Chapter 10 lists it in the non-forming combinations table. Expect 1-2 exam questions on 'which combination does NOT form a channel?' — passive+passive is the textbook answer.

2.4.b.info:
active + active = channel forms. Both sides initiate LACP, both send LACPDUs immediately upon link-up, both verify each other's system IDs, and the channel bundles within roughly 30 seconds (one LACPDU interval).

Why this is the recommended combination: it's the most robust. There's no ambiguity, no waiting, no 'who speaks first' dance. If both sides are shouting hello simultaneously, they hear each other. If one side reboots, it immediately re-initiates as soon as it comes back up, and the channel rebuilds fast. Both-active is the bulletproof default.

In exam questions: 'Switch A is configured with channel-group 1 mode active. Switch B is configured with channel-group 1 mode active. Will the EtherChannel form?' → Yes.

Configuration reminder:

! Switch A
interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 channel-group 1 mode active
!
! Switch B
interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 channel-group 1 mode active

Channel group numbers don't need to match across switches (they're locally significant), but the mode must match the protocol family: both sides LACP active, not one side LACP active and the other PAgP desirable.

hack.memory: Active + Active = two extroverts saying hi at the same time = instant conversation. Always works. Fastest, safest combo. If you can only remember ONE EtherChannel command for the exam and real life: 'channel-group 1 mode active' on both sides. Done.
hack.practice: Configure in Packet Tracer: both sides 'mode active'. Run 'show etherchannel summary' within 30s — channel is (SU) and both ports are (P). Time to formation is the key takeaway — active+active is the fastest combo.
hack.meta: Exam-guaranteed question. OCG Chapter 10 and Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 both emphasize active+active as the preferred combo. If asked 'recommended configuration?' the answer is 'LACP, both sides active.'

2.4.b.info:
active + passive = channel forms. The active side initiates by sending LACPDUs. The passive side, upon receiving those LACPDUs, responds with its own LACPDUs. Both sides then complete normal LACP negotiation and the channel bundles.

Why it still works: the 'at least one active' rule is satisfied. Only one side needs to be the conversation starter. The passive side is a good responder — it just refuses to initiate. As soon as the active side breaks the silence, the passive side participates fully.

When you'd see this in the wild: sometimes a network operator uses passive on one side as a safety measure — for example, on a port facing a host or non-switch device. If the other end turns out to be an LACP-capable switch configured as active, the channel forms; if not, the passive port stays quiet rather than sending unsolicited LACPDUs to a confused host.

Exam wording: 'Switch A is mode active, Switch B is mode passive. Will the channel form?' → Yes.

Timing note: active+passive forms slightly slower than active+active because the passive side has to wait for the first LACPDU to arrive before starting its own transmissions. The difference is 1-2 LACPDU intervals (30-60 seconds in slow mode), not huge.

hack.memory: Active + Passive = extrovert asks, introvert answers = conversation happens. Works because the rule is 'at least one active.' Passive can carry its weight, it just won't start the dance.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: Switch A 'mode active', Switch B 'mode passive'. Verify channel forms. Then reverse — A passive, B active — still forms. Prove the symmetry: doesn't matter which side is active, as long as ONE side is.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10 matrix. Exam-standard combination to recognize as 'forms'. Often paired with 'passive+passive' (doesn't form) in compare-and-contrast questions.

2.4.b.info:
passive + passive = channel does NOT form. Both sides wait for the other to initiate LACPDUs. Neither one ever does. The ports stay standalone (I) or blank in show etherchannel summary, and no amount of waiting will fix it — only a configuration change will.

Why this is THE classic exam trap: engineers sometimes think 'I'll configure both sides identically to be safe' and set both to passive. The configurations look symmetric and correct, but the channel never bundles. The symptom: physical links up, ports in individual (I) state, no LACP neighbor detected on either side.

Diagnosis: show etherchannel summary shows no port-channel (or a port-channel with all members in I state). show lacp neighbor shows nothing — no partner detected. show running-config reveals both sides configured as passive.

The fix: change at least one side to active. The moment one side starts transmitting LACPDUs, the passive side responds and the channel bundles within 30 seconds.

Exam phrasing: 'Both switches are configured with channel-group 1 mode passive. What happens?' → The channel does not form. Neither switch initiates LACP negotiation.

hack.memory: Passive + Passive = two wallflowers at a dance, both waiting to be asked. Dance never happens. Most common EtherChannel exam trap. If you see both sides set to passive — answer is always 'no channel forms.' Fix: one side must be active.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure both sides as 'mode passive'. Run 'show etherchannel summary' — ports are (I) or the channel is missing entirely. Wait 5 minutes — still nothing. Change one side to 'mode active' — channel forms within 30 seconds. This is the most memorable EtherChannel lab you can do.
hack.meta: Almost certainly on the exam. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 labs it explicitly. OCG Chapter 10 flags it as the #1 gotcha. Sister trap: auto+auto in PAgP. Know both.

2.4.b.info:
LACP supports up to 16 configured physical links in a single channel group, but only up to 8 can be active simultaneously. The remaining 8 are placed in hot-standby state — physically up, LACP exchanging, but not forwarding traffic until they're needed.

How standby selection works: each port has an LACP port priority (default 32768, range 0-65535, lower is better). LACP ranks all 16 configured ports by priority and activates the 8 with the best (lowest-numeric) priorities. The other 8 sit in hot-standby waiting. If an active port fails, the next-best standby port is activated automatically — much faster than waiting for a brand-new port to negotiate from scratch.

The standby flag: standby ports show the 'H' flag in show etherchannel summary (hot-standby), while active bundled ports show (P). So a channel with 16 configured members might display 8 ports as (P) and 8 as (H).

Why 16 configured instead of just 8: it's a form of rapid redundancy. If you configured only 8 ports and one failed, the channel drops to 7 (reduced bandwidth) and stays there until a human re-cables. With 16 configured and 8 active, a failure promotes a standby to active automatically — the channel stays at full 8-port capacity.

PAgP comparison: PAgP only supports 8 configured ports max, no standby concept. This is one of the few concrete advantages LACP has over PAgP beyond the open-standard question.

Configuration for priority (optional): lacp port-priority [value] on a member interface. Lower values are preferred for active selection.

transitions: config → negotiating → bundled if priority allows
bundled member fails → standby promotes automatically

hack.memory: LACP: 16 configured, 8 active, 8 hot-standby. Like a starting lineup of 8 on the field + 8 on the bench ready to sub in instantly. Flag H = on the bench. Lower port-priority = 'better athlete' = gets on the field. PAgP: 8 max, no bench.
hack.practice: Configure 9+ member ports (hypothetically — most Packet Tracer switches don't have 9 same-speed ports, but you can still read the theory). Understand that you'd see 8 (P) bundled and the rest (H) hot-standby. The concept matters more than the lab for this point.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 mentions the 16/8 rule briefly. OCG Chapter 10 states it plainly. Expected exam question: 'what is the maximum number of active links in an LACP EtherChannel?' → 8. 'Maximum configured?' → 16.

2.4.c.info:
PAgP (Port Aggregation Protocol) is Cisco's proprietary EtherChannel negotiation protocol. It predates LACP and served as the original Cisco solution for dynamic EtherChannel formation. Functionally, it does the same job as LACP: negotiate the channel, detect misconfigurations, monitor for partner failures, and manage member ports. Only Cisco devices understand PAgP.

Why PAgP still exists: legacy. Many enterprise networks deployed PAgP years before LACP matured on Cisco gear. Those deployments still work and haven't been migrated. PAgP also has slightly different messaging semantics that some engineers preferred in the early 2000s. For any new deployment, LACP is the industry-standard choice — PAgP is maintenance-only.

Protocol mechanics: PAgP sends its own negotiation frames (not LACPDUs, but Cisco's own format — they use a Cisco-specific multicast destination address 0100.0CCC.CCCC and SNAP encapsulation with Cisco's OUI). The frames carry information about the sending port, the channel group, and the mode (desirable or auto). Each side validates the partner and either forms the channel or keeps the ports standalone.

Cross-vendor reality: connect a Cisco switch to an Arista, Juniper, Dell, HP, or Linux server — PAgP will not work. The non-Cisco side won't understand PAgP frames at all. You must use LACP (or static) for any non-Cisco partner.

Exam expectation: know what PAgP is, know it's Cisco-only, know the two modes (desirable and auto), and know the mode mapping to LACP (desirable=active, auto=passive). The exam mentions PAgP primarily to contrast it with LACP.

hack.memory: PAgP = 'Private Aggregation Program' — Cisco's clubhouse. Cisco-only. Same concept as LACP, different names (desirable/auto instead of active/passive). If the other end isn't Cisco, PAgP is impossible — use LACP. Remember: PAgP is the 'P' letter — think 'Proprietary.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure 'channel-group 1 mode desirable' both sides (PAgP). Verify 'show etherchannel summary' — protocol column shows 'PAgP'. Run 'show pagp neighbor' — same info as 'show lacp neighbor' but in PAgP format. Then mix — one side LACP active, other side PAgP desirable — channel fails to form because protocols don't match.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 covers PAgP alongside LACP. OCG Chapter 10 presents them as equals. Exam question: 'which EtherChannel protocol is Cisco proprietary?' → PAgP. 'Which is IEEE standard?' → LACP.

2.4.c.info:
PAgP desirable mode configures a port to actively initiate PAgP negotiation. A desirable port sends PAgP frames as soon as the link comes up, trying to form a channel with the partner. Command: channel-group N mode desirable.

LACP equivalent: desirable = active. Both modes mean 'I will initiate.' The semantic is identical; only the protocol (PAgP vs LACP) and the word used differ. If you understand LACP active, you understand PAgP desirable.

Rule: for a PAgP channel to form, at least one side must be desirable. Exactly parallel to 'at least one side must be active' in LACP. Two sides in auto mode will never form a channel because neither will initiate.

Configuration example:

! Switch A (Cisco only)
interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 channel-group 1 mode desirable
!
! Switch B (Cisco only)
interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 channel-group 1 mode desirable

Both sides desirable is the PAgP equivalent of both sides active — the fastest and most robust PAgP combination.

Verification: show etherchannel summary shows protocol 'PAgP' and members as (P) when bundled. show pagp neighbor shows the partner's info.

hack.memory: PAgP Desirable = LACP Active = 'I want to form this channel NOW.' The initiator. Mnemonic: 'Desirable people take Action' — desirable maps to active. Command: 'channel-group 1 mode desirable' — Cisco-only flavor.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: both sides 'mode desirable'. Verify channel forms. Mirror the LACP active+active lab but with PAgP syntax. Then try desirable+auto — also forms.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10 lists the mode pairs. Exam asks the mode-mapping question frequently: 'PAgP desirable is equivalent to which LACP mode?' → active.

2.4.c.info:
PAgP auto mode configures a port to only respond to PAgP negotiation. An auto port does not send unsolicited PAgP frames — it waits until the partner's PAgP frames arrive, then responds. Command: channel-group N mode auto.

LACP equivalent: auto = passive. Same behavior — 'I will respond but not initiate.' If you know LACP passive, you know PAgP auto.

The classic trap: auto + auto = NO channel. Both sides wait for the other to start, neither does, channel never forms. Exactly parallel to LACP passive+passive. The fix is identical: change at least one side to the initiating mode (desirable).

Real-world use: auto is rarely used as a deliberate choice. It sometimes appears when an engineer wants a 'quiet' port that doesn't send PAgP unless prompted, but active/desirable on both sides is almost always better. If you inherit an environment with auto+auto, it's usually a misconfiguration.

Verification when paired with desirable: show pagp neighbor on the auto side will show the partner's flags as 'D' (desirable) and your own flags as 'A' (auto). The channel bundles normally once the auto side has received a PAgP frame.

hack.memory: PAgP Auto = LACP Passive = 'I'll respond if you ask.' Mnemonic: 'Auto people are Passive' — auto maps to passive. Auto+Auto = two wallflowers = NO channel, same as passive+passive.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: both sides 'mode auto' — verify channel does NOT form. Change one side to 'mode desirable' — verify it now forms. Parallel the passive+passive lab you already did for LACP. This reinforces the mode-mapping.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Exam question style: 'Switch A is auto, Switch B is auto. Does the PAgP EtherChannel form?' → No. Sister trap to passive+passive.

2.4.c.info:
desirable + desirable = channel forms. Both Cisco switches initiate PAgP, both send PAgP frames, both respond, and the channel bundles. This is the preferred PAgP configuration — the equivalent of LACP active+active.

desirable + auto = channel forms. The desirable side initiates with PAgP frames. The auto side, upon receiving them, responds. The channel bundles through normal negotiation. Slightly slower than desirable+desirable because the auto side waits for the first frame, but still works. Equivalent of LACP active+passive.

The underlying rule: at least one side must be desirable for a PAgP channel to form. This is the PAgP version of the LACP 'at least one active' rule. Same concept, different word.

Exam wording: 'Switch A is desirable, Switch B is auto. Will the channel form?' → Yes. 'Switch A is desirable, Switch B is desirable. Will the channel form?' → Yes. These two combinations always succeed.

Mirror table with LACP:

  • LACP active+active → PAgP desirable+desirable (both work)
  • LACP active+passive → PAgP desirable+auto (both work)
  • LACP passive+passive → PAgP auto+auto (both FAIL)

hack.memory: PAgP YES combos: Desirable+Desirable, Desirable+Auto. One initiator (desirable) is enough. Mirror of LACP's active+active and active+passive. If you've memorized the LACP matrix, you already know PAgP — just swap words.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: two test runs. (1) Both desirable — forms. (2) Desirable + auto — forms. Pair each with the LACP equivalent lab to cement the mode mapping. Compare 'show etherchannel summary' — the only difference is the protocol column (PAgP vs LACP); the flags and behavior are identical.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Guaranteed exam topic — expect the matrix question in some form.

2.4.c.info:
auto + auto = channel does NOT form. Both PAgP ports wait for the other to initiate. Neither one does. The ports stay standalone (I) or blank in show etherchannel summary and will never bundle without a configuration change. This is the direct PAgP parallel to LACP's passive+passive trap.

Diagnosis: same symptoms as passive+passive in LACP. Physical links up, ports not bundled, no PAgP neighbor detected. show pagp neighbor returns empty on both sides. show running-config shows both sides in mode auto.

The fix: change at least one side to desirable. Once one side starts transmitting PAgP frames, the auto side will respond and the channel forms within a few seconds.

Why this trap exists: in the 2000s, auto was sometimes the default mode for reasons of 'don't send PAgP unless asked.' An engineer migrating or re-creating a config might inadvertently leave both sides as auto. The channel never forms, and because there are no error messages (auto is a valid config — it's just silent), troubleshooting takes a while.

Exam framing: 'Switch A is mode auto, Switch B is mode auto. Does the PAgP EtherChannel form?' → No. 'What is the fix?' → Change at least one side to mode desirable.

hack.memory: Auto+Auto = Passive+Passive = two wallflowers. Classic exam trap. If the exam gives you both-auto or both-passive, the answer is ALWAYS 'no channel.' Fix is always 'make at least one side desirable/active.'
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: both sides 'mode auto'. Verify no channel forms. Then change Switch A to 'mode desirable' — verify channel now forms. This parallels the LACP passive+passive → one-side-active lab.
hack.meta: Exam trap #2 (after passive+passive). OCG Chapter 10. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. Learn both traps together — they're the same concept in two protocol dialects.

2.4.c.info:
LACP and PAgP are conceptually identical — same two-mode structure, same 'at least one initiator' rule. The exam tests whether you know the mapping between the two protocols' mode names.

The mapping:

  • LACP active ≡ PAgP desirable — both actively initiate negotiation frames
  • LACP passive ≡ PAgP auto — both only respond; never initiate

Memory trick: 'DA = PA.' Desirable = Active. Auto = Passive. Or alphabetically: 'Active-Desirable, Passive-Auto' — both pairs go together.

Critical rule: protocols CANNOT be mixed. If Switch A uses LACP (active or passive) and Switch B uses PAgP (desirable or auto), the channel does not form. The two sides send different negotiation frames and don't understand each other. Both sides must use the same protocol family:

  • Both LACP (active/passive) — works if at least one is active
  • Both PAgP (desirable/auto) — works if at least one is desirable
  • Both static (mode on) — works
  • Any mix of protocols — fails

Common scenario: an engineer inherits a Cisco switch with PAgP and replaces the partner switch with a non-Cisco switch. The new partner is configured with LACP because that's all it supports. The channel won't form on either side until the Cisco switch is changed from PAgP to LACP.

hack.memory: DA = PA. Desirable = Active. Auto = Passive. Write it on the exam whiteboard. Also: 'protocols don't mix' — LACP paired with PAgP = no channel. Same protocol family on both sides, or nothing.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure Switch A with LACP active, Switch B with PAgP desirable. Verify the channel does NOT form (show etherchannel summary shows ports as standalone or the channel in error). Then align both sides to LACP active — channel forms. This demonstrates the 'no mixing' rule concretely.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 shows the mapping table. OCG Chapter 10 lists both the mapping and the 'no mixing' rule. Expected exam questions: 'PAgP desirable maps to which LACP mode?' → active. 'Can LACP and PAgP be mixed across a link?' → No.

2.4.d.info:
Static EtherChannel (also called 'mode on' or just 'unconditional EtherChannel') forces the port-channel to form without any negotiation protocol. No LACP frames, no PAgP frames — the switch simply bundles the configured member ports based purely on local configuration. Both sides must be configured identically as channel-group N mode on.

How it behaves: the moment a member interface is configured with channel-group 1 mode on and the physical link comes up, the switch adds that port to Port-channel 1. No handshake, no verification that the partner is even configured for EtherChannel. If the local config says 'bundle it,' the switch bundles it.

Verification: show etherchannel summary shows the protocol column as '-' (no protocol) for static channels, as opposed to 'LACP' or 'PAgP' for dynamic channels. Member ports still show (P) when bundled.

Why engineers sometimes use it: mainly historical or in environments with devices that don't support any negotiation protocol. Some older network equipment or some non-switch hosts only speak static aggregation. A rare modern use is extremely controlled lab environments where the engineer wants zero negotiation overhead. But in 2026, on any modern Cisco gear paired with anything that speaks LACP, use LACP.

Syntax:

interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 channel-group 1 mode on

Identical on both sides. No other options — no active/passive/desirable/auto equivalents, no sub-modes. Just 'on.'

hack.memory: Mode on = 'duct tape EtherChannel.' Holds links together without checking anything. No LACP, no PAgP, no negotiation, no safety net. Only on+on works — nothing else pairs with it. Exam answer to 'which mode uses no negotiation protocol?' = on.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure both sides 'channel-group 1 mode on'. Verify 'show etherchannel summary' — protocol column shows '-' (dash, indicating no protocol). Channel is up. Then run 'show lacp neighbor' and 'show pagp neighbor' — both return empty because no negotiation is happening.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 covers mode on. OCG Chapter 10 warns against it in modern deployments. The exam asks: 'which EtherChannel mode does not use a negotiation protocol?' → on (static).

2.4.d.info:
The major danger of mode on is no error detection. Because no negotiation occurs, the switch cannot verify that the partner is configured correctly. If one side is mode on and the other side is misconfigured (wrong channel group, no EtherChannel at all, or a different number of bundled ports), the static side will still think it has a working channel and forward traffic accordingly. The result can be a Layer 2 loop.

The loop scenario: Switch A has Gi0/1 and Gi0/2 configured as channel-group 1 mode on. Switch B has Gi0/1 and Gi0/2 connected but NOT configured for EtherChannel (they're individual trunks). Switch A happily bundles its two ports into Po1 and load-balances traffic across them. Switch B sees two independent ports, and because they're not in an EtherChannel, STP would normally block one of them — but STP sees BPDUs arriving on both ports from Switch A's single logical Po1, which confuses STP's loop-prevention logic. You end up with packets getting duplicated or looped because STP can't properly arbitrate.

Why LACP/PAgP prevent this: with LACP or PAgP, the negotiation process requires the partner to acknowledge the channel formation. If Switch B doesn't send LACP frames, Switch A's LACP ports stay individual (I) and don't bundle. No channel = no loop risk. LACP's handshake IS the error detection.

Compounding problem: mode on also has no partner-failure detection. If Switch B's CPU crashes but physical links stay up, Switch A's mode-on channel keeps forwarding traffic into a black hole. LACP would detect missing LACPDUs within 90 seconds and remove the affected member, but mode on has no such mechanism.

Best practice: always use LACP. Use mode on only if you literally cannot use LACP or PAgP (extremely rare on modern gear).

right.label: LACP (safe)
items: Channel stays individual if partner is wrong

hack.memory: Mode on risk = 'driving without looking.' No verification, no detection, pure trust in the local config. If one side is wrong, you might get L2 loops — the exact thing EtherChannel is supposed to prevent. Always use LACP active unless something literally forces you not to.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure Switch A with 'mode on' on both member ports. Leave Switch B's ports as plain trunks without any channel-group config. Watch the chaos: Switch A thinks it has a bundled channel, Switch B doesn't, BPDUs arrive on both Switch A ports, and you may see MAC address flapping in 'show mac address-table.' Then reconfigure both sides to 'mode active' (LACP) — the channel either forms cleanly or stays individual, never the dangerous middle state.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 warns about this. OCG Chapter 10 calls mode on 'not recommended.' The exam may phrase it as: 'what is a risk of using static EtherChannel (mode on)?' → potential for Layer 2 loops due to no error detection.

2.4.d.info:
Mode on cannot be paired with ANY negotiation mode. The complete incompatibility matrix:

  • on + on = YES (the ONLY working combination with on)
  • on + active = NO
  • on + passive = NO
  • on + desirable = NO
  • on + auto = NO

Why: mode on bundles unconditionally based on local config. It doesn't send LACP or PAgP frames, so the negotiation-mode side never gets the handshake it's waiting for. Meanwhile, the negotiation side keeps trying to negotiate but gets silence back. The result: the negotiation side keeps its ports as standalone (I), waiting forever. Meanwhile, the mode-on side still thinks it has a channel — and may forward traffic into the negotiation side's individual ports, creating the loop scenario described in the risk card.

Rule of thumb: mode on is a loner. It only partners with another mode on. Any time you see a mode on paired with anything else in a question, the answer is 'does not form.'

Common exam phrasing: 'Switch A is configured as mode on, Switch B is configured as mode active. Will the EtherChannel form?' → No. This is the classic 'mixing static and dynamic' trap.

Real-world implication: if you're migrating from a static channel to LACP, you must change both sides at the same time. Changing only one side leaves you in a broken state (mode on paired with mode active = no channel = outage). Schedule a maintenance window and coordinate the change on both switches.

hack.memory: Mode on = 'the loner.' Only partners with itself. on+on = YES. Everything else with on = NO. If the exam shows mode on paired with active/passive/desirable/auto, the answer is always 'does not form.' During migrations, change both sides simultaneously.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: Switch A mode on, Switch B mode active — verify channel does NOT form. Switch A mode on, Switch B mode on — forms. Switch A mode active, Switch B mode active — forms (LACP). This triple-lab cements the incompatibility rules.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10 matrix. Guaranteed exam topic — the 'mode on + mode active' incompatibility is a common distractor in multiple-choice questions. Answer: no channel.

2.4.d.info:
Honest answer: almost never. In 2026, there is essentially no good reason to use static EtherChannel on modern Cisco gear. LACP is universally supported, safer, and the industry standard.

The narrow edge cases where mode on might be justified:

  • Legacy equipment that doesn't support LACP or PAgP. Extremely rare on modern gear. Some older non-Cisco devices from the early 2000s lacked LACP support. If you're maintaining a network with such equipment, static is your only option. But those devices are nearly extinct.
  • Extremely controlled lab environments where you want to eliminate negotiation as a variable for testing. This is a niche use case — most labs use LACP because it models production.
  • Specialized lab-only testing of 'what happens when I misconfigure one side?' You'd use static on one side to demonstrate the risk of no error detection. This is a teaching use, not a production use.

What to do instead: use LACP active on both sides. This is the universally-correct default answer for the exam and the real world. It gives you error detection, partner-failure detection, multi-vendor compatibility, and the 16/8 hot-standby model. No downsides compared to static.

Exam question: 'When should you use mode on?' The Cisco-approved answer: 'only for devices that don't support LACP or PAgP.' In practice, that means 'never on modern gear.'

hack.memory: When to use mode on? Almost never. Default answer: use LACP active on both sides. Mode on is a historical artifact — only useful when the partner is too old/limited to speak any negotiation protocol. If anyone offers you static EtherChannel in 2026, ask why.
hack.practice: No practice needed beyond the labs you've already done. The lesson is architectural: when you design a new EtherChannel, always pick LACP. Writing 'channel-group 1 mode active' should be muscle memory.
hack.meta: Weight: low. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 mentions but doesn't emphasize this. OCG Chapter 10 treats mode on as deprecated. The exam touches on it only to contrast with LACP. Expect at most one question.

2.4.e.info:
The combinations that successfully form an EtherChannel. Memorize this list — it's the 'will it form?' side of the negotiation matrix:

  • LACP: active + active — both initiate, channel forms fast.
  • LACP: active + passive — one initiates, the other responds, channel forms.
  • PAgP: desirable + desirable — both initiate, channel forms.
  • PAgP: desirable + auto — one initiates, the other responds, channel forms.
  • Static: on + on — no negotiation, both sides bundle unconditionally.

The common thread: within each protocol, at least one side must be the initiator. For LACP that's 'active,' for PAgP that's 'desirable,' for static both must be on (there's no initiator/responder distinction because there's no negotiation).

What the list does NOT include: no LACP-to-PAgP combinations, no mode-on-to-negotiation combinations. Those are all on the NO side. Both sides must be in the same protocol family and must have at least one initiator.

Exam recall tip: write these five combos on your whiteboard at the start of the exam. Any 'will it form?' question can be answered instantly by matching to this list. If the combo is on this list → YES. If not → NO.

hack.memory: Five YES combos to memorize: A+A, A+P, D+D, D+Au, on+on. (active/passive = LACP, desirable/auto = PAgP). Rule: 'at least one initiator per protocol.' Write these on your exam whiteboard first thing.
hack.practice: Drill with flashcards: shuffle mode pairs and answer YES/NO in under 2 seconds. Use a partner if possible. Goal: instant recognition.
hack.meta: Exam-critical. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 lists the matrix. OCG Chapter 10 has the table verbatim. Expect 1-2 'will this combination form?' questions — usually one YES and one NO.

2.4.e.info:
The combinations that do NOT form an EtherChannel. This is the trap side of the matrix — know these cold because they're the most-tested exam scenarios:

  • LACP: passive + passive — both wait for the other, neither initiates, channel never forms.
  • PAgP: auto + auto — same problem, PAgP flavor.
  • on + active — mode on can't negotiate; active side waits for LACPDUs that never arrive; channel fails (and the on side may cause loops).
  • on + passive — same failure as above.
  • on + desirable — same failure.
  • on + auto — same failure.
  • Any LACP mode + any PAgP mode — different protocols, no shared language, no channel. (active+desirable = NO, active+auto = NO, passive+desirable = NO, passive+auto = NO.)

The two underlying reasons for failure:

  1. No initiator: two responder-only modes in the same protocol (passive+passive, auto+auto). Neither side speaks first, both wait forever.
  2. Protocol mismatch: one side uses LACP, the other uses PAgP, or one side uses static mode on and the other uses any negotiation mode. Frames don't match.

Exam recall tip: 'same protocol + at least one initiator' is the rule for YES. Any violation of that rule is a NO.

hack.memory: NO combos: passive+passive, auto+auto, on + anything-not-on, LACP-mixed-with-PAgP. Two rules to violate: 'no initiator' or 'protocol mismatch.' If either applies = NO channel.
hack.practice: Flashcard drill alongside the YES list. For each NO combo, identify WHICH rule is violated (no initiator vs protocol mismatch) — this deepens understanding beyond rote memorization.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Exam-critical. The NO side is tested MORE than the YES side because it's the trap — expect at least one 'which combo does not form?' question, often passive+passive or on+active.

2.4.e.info:
The at-least-one-initiator rule is the single principle that governs all negotiation-based EtherChannels: within a given protocol, at least one side must be configured in the initiating mode. In LACP that means at least one side must be active. In PAgP that means at least one side must be desirable.

Why the rule exists: negotiation requires someone to speak first. Responder modes (LACP passive, PAgP auto) explicitly refuse to initiate — they only react to incoming negotiation frames. If both sides are responders, neither speaks, and the conversation never starts. The channel sits in an eternal waiting state.

The three valid patterns that satisfy the rule:

  1. Both sides initiate (active+active, desirable+desirable) — fastest formation, most robust.
  2. One side initiates, one responds (active+passive, desirable+auto) — works, slightly slower.
  3. Neither initiates but both are static (on+on) — no negotiation at all, channel forms unconditionally. The rule doesn't apply because there's no negotiation.

The invalid patterns: both sides responder (passive+passive or auto+auto) — violates the rule, no channel.

Exam framing: sometimes the exam asks a conceptual question rather than a specific combo: 'What must be true for an LACP EtherChannel to form?' → At least one side must be in active mode.

transitions: initiator sends first frame → responder replies → negotiation completes → channel bundles

hack.memory: At-least-one-initiator rule: someone has to speak first. Active/desirable = speakers. Passive/auto = listeners. Two listeners = silent room = no channel. Exception: mode on bypasses this entirely because there's no conversation — just unilateral bundling.
hack.practice: Verbal drill: for each combo, identify if the rule is satisfied. Example: 'active + passive' → one speaker, rule satisfied, forms. 'passive + passive' → zero speakers, rule violated, fails. Do this until it's automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 emphasizes this rule. OCG Chapter 10 states it plainly. The exam loves conceptual framings of this rule — 'what condition must be met for the channel to form?' answer: at least one active/desirable side.

2.4.e.info:
Rule: both sides of an EtherChannel must use the same protocol family. You cannot mix LACP with PAgP. You cannot mix either with mode on. The protocols speak different negotiation languages and don't understand each other.

Why it fails: LACP sends LACPDUs (IEEE format). PAgP sends its own Cisco-specific frames. Mode on sends neither. If Switch A sends LACPDUs and Switch B is listening for PAgP frames, Switch B never sees anything it recognizes. Both sides time out waiting for the 'right' frame type, and the channel never forms. The ports stay individual (I) indefinitely.

The complete incompatibility list (cross-protocol):

  • LACP active + PAgP desirable = NO
  • LACP active + PAgP auto = NO
  • LACP passive + PAgP desirable = NO
  • LACP passive + PAgP auto = NO
  • LACP (any) + mode on = NO
  • PAgP (any) + mode on = NO

The only valid protocol combinations:

  • Both LACP (any valid LACP combo from the YES list)
  • Both PAgP (any valid PAgP combo from the YES list)
  • Both mode on

Real-world scenario: you're migrating a Cisco-Cisco link from PAgP to LACP. You cannot do this by changing one side at a time while maintaining the channel — as soon as Switch A is LACP and Switch B is still PAgP, the channel dies. You must either (a) accept a brief outage and change both sides simultaneously, or (b) temporarily bring the channel down, change both sides, bring it up.

Exam-typical wording: 'Switch A uses LACP active, Switch B uses PAgP desirable. Will the EtherChannel form?' → No. Protocols cannot be mixed.

items: LACP must pair with LACP only
PAgP must pair with PAgP only
mode on must pair with mode on only

hack.memory: Never mix protocols. LACP talks LACP, PAgP talks PAgP, mode on talks to itself. Cross-protocol = no common language = no channel. During migrations, change both sides at once or schedule an outage.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: Switch A LACP active, Switch B PAgP desirable. Verify 'show etherchannel summary' — ports standalone (I), no channel formed. Align both to LACP active — channel forms. This demonstrates the rule experientially.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Exam-likely: cross-protocol combos in multiple-choice distractors. Answer: they don't work. Both sides must match the protocol family.

2.4.f.info:
Layer 2 EtherChannel bundles ports that are configured as switchports (either access or trunk). The resulting port-channel interface operates at Layer 2 — it has no IP address of its own, it forwards frames based on MAC addresses and VLAN tags, and it participates in STP and other L2 protocols as a single logical interface.

When you use it: switch-to-switch uplinks between access/distribution/core layers. Access ports bundled together to a host (rare — usually handled at L3 with NIC teaming). Trunk uplinks carrying multiple VLANs between two switches. Basically any bundled L2 link in a switched network.

Configuration basics:

! Switch A
interface range Gi0/1 - 4
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
 channel-group 1 mode active
!
interface Port-channel 1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30

Notice that the trunk configuration appears on both the range and the Port-channel interface. In practice, configuring on the Port-channel interface first and then adding members is cleaner — the port-channel pushes its config down to members automatically. But you can also configure members first and then channel-group them; IOS will sync them.

Verification: show etherchannel summary shows the channel with SU flag (Switch layer, in Use). Members show (P) when bundled. show interfaces trunk shows the port-channel as a trunk interface carrying the allowed VLANs. show spanning-tree shows Po1 as a single STP interface.

hack.memory: L2 EtherChannel = 'one trunk cable made of 4 threads.' Switchport-configured, carries VLANs, participates in STP. Flag = SU (Switch layer, in Use). Members = (P) when bundled. Configure trunk settings on Port-channel interface for cleanest config.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build an L2 trunk EtherChannel carrying VLANs 10, 20, 30. Configure on the Port-channel interface first. Verify 'show etherchannel summary' shows SU/P. Verify 'show interfaces trunk' lists the Port-channel as a trunk. Verify 'show spanning-tree' shows only Po1, not the individual members.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 primarily uses L2 EtherChannels. OCG Chapter 10 presents L2 as the default scenario. Exam simlets often require configuring an L2 trunk EtherChannel between two switches with specific VLANs allowed.

2.4.f.info:
Best practice: configure trunk, VLAN, access, and STP settings on the Port-channel interface, not on the individual member interfaces. The Port-channel pushes its configuration automatically down to all members, keeping everything synchronized and preventing the configuration drift that causes port suspension.

How the push-down works: when you type switchport mode trunk on interface Port-channel 1, IOS immediately copies that line into the config of every member port (Gi0/1, Gi0/2, etc.). Check with show running-config interface Gi0/1 — you'll see the trunk configuration there, pushed down from Po1. This happens for access VLANs, allowed VLAN lists, native VLANs, STP cost, STP priority, and most other L2 settings.

What to configure on members directly (rarely): physical-layer settings like speed, duplex, and description can go on the member interfaces. But even those are usually best left to auto-negotiation. The only thing you must configure on the member directly is the channel-group command itself — that's how you assign the port to the channel.

Why this matters: configuration drift (one member has different settings than the others) causes port suspension ('s' flag). The easiest way to avoid drift is to never touch the members directly after the initial channel-group assignment. Let the Port-channel interface be the single source of truth.

Typical workflow:

! Step 1: assign members to channel group
interface range Gi0/1 - 4
 channel-group 1 mode active
!
! Step 2: configure the Port-channel (auto-pushed to members)
interface Port-channel 1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
 switchport trunk native vlan 99
 spanning-tree guard root

hack.memory: Port-channel interface = 'the team manager.' Give the manager one set of rules, the manager hands them to every player. Configure on Po1, IOS pushes to members. Never configure trunk/VLAN on members directly — that's how you get config drift and suspended ports.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: create an LACP channel. Configure 'switchport mode trunk' + 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20' on interface Port-channel 1. Run 'show run interface Gi0/1' — verify the trunk config appears there too. That's the auto-push. Now manually change Gi0/1's allowed VLAN list to something different — watch the port get suspended ('s' flag in summary). Fix by putting the correct list back or removing the member-level config.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 emphasizes this best practice. OCG Chapter 10. Exam typical phrasing: 'where should trunk/VLAN settings be configured for an EtherChannel?' → on the Port-channel interface.

2.4.f.info:
show etherchannel summary is the primary verification command for EtherChannels. It produces a compact output showing every port-channel on the switch, the protocol in use, the channel's state flags, and the state of each member port.

Example healthy L2 output:

Flags:  D - down        P - bundled in port-channel
        I - stand-alone s - suspended
        H - Hot-standby (LACP only)
        R - Layer3      S - Layer2
        U - in use      f - failed to allocate aggregator

Number of channel-groups in use: 1
Number of aggregators:           1

Group  Port-channel  Protocol    Ports
------+-------------+-----------+-----------------------
1      Po1(SU)       LACP        Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

Parsing the output:

  • Po1(SU) — port-channel 1, Switch (L2), in Use. Healthy L2 channel.
  • LACP — the negotiation protocol. For PAgP it says 'PAgP'. For static it says '-' (dash).
  • Gi0/1(P) ... Gi0/4(P) — each member port is bundled in the port-channel. Healthy state.

L3 healthy output would show Po1(RU) instead of (SU), and other flags are explained in the port-level flags card (2.4.i).

Use this command every time you make an EtherChannel change. It's fast, comprehensive, and answers 'did my channel come up?' in one line. If something's wrong (suspended port, down channel, standalone ports), you'll see it immediately in the flags.

hack.memory: show etherchannel summary = 'the EtherChannel dashboard.' First command after any EC config change. Read left to right: Group number, Po flag (SU/RU/SD/RD), Protocol, Member ports and their flags. Healthy = SU or RU on the channel, (P) on every member.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, run 'show etherchannel summary' after: (1) creating a new channel, (2) bringing down a member, (3) bringing down all members, (4) misconfiguring a member. Each scenario produces a distinct output. Memorize the look of a healthy output so anomalies jump out.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 uses this command constantly. OCG Chapter 10 includes sample outputs. Exam simlets display this output and ask 'what's wrong?' — being able to parse it instantly is a key exam skill.

2.4.f.info:
The 's' flag (lowercase s) means a member port is suspended from the port-channel because its configuration does not match the other members. The port is physically up and the link is fine — but the switch refuses to bundle it until you fix the mismatch.

What causes suspension: any of the SDVTS mismatches — Speed, Duplex, VLAN, Trunk config (mode/encapsulation/allowed list/native), or STP settings. The most common culprits are VLAN and trunk allowed-list mismatches, followed by STP cost differences.

How to diagnose:

  1. Identify the suspended port — look for '(s)' in show etherchannel summary. Example: Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(s) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P) — Gi0/2 is suspended.
  2. Compare configurations — run show running-config interface Gi0/2 and show running-config interface Gi0/1 side by side. Look for any setting that differs.
  3. Check specific areas — access VLAN (show interfaces Gi0/2 switchport), trunk allowed list, speed/duplex (show interfaces Gi0/2 for actual vs configured), STP cost.
  4. Fix the mismatch — edit the offending port's config to match the others, or (better) remove the member-level override and let the Port-channel interface push the correct config back down.

Recovery behavior: once you fix the mismatch, the port automatically re-bundles. No shut/no-shut is needed. Within a few seconds, show etherchannel summary will show the port back at (P).

hack.memory: 's' flag = 'suspended' = 'out of uniform.' The port is physically up but benched. Cause is always a config mismatch — check Speed, Duplex, VLAN, Trunk config, STP. Fix the mismatch and the port auto-rejoins, no shut/no-shut.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build a 4-port EtherChannel, all (P). Change Gi0/2's access VLAN to something different from the others. Watch Gi0/2 go to (s). Revert the VLAN change. Watch Gi0/2 return to (P) automatically. Repeat with a trunk allowed-list mismatch. This builds the 'mismatch → suspended' instinct that exam simlets test.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 labs suspension. OCG Chapter 10. Exam simlets show 's' in the summary output and ask for the cause — answer: configuration mismatch. The most tested troubleshooting scenario in EtherChannel.

2.4.f.info:
The 'I' flag (uppercase I, for Individual/stand-alone) means a member port is not bundled into the port-channel — it's operating as a standalone interface. This usually indicates a negotiation failure rather than a config mismatch.

Common causes:

  • Mode mismatch across the link — LACP passive + passive (no initiator), or PAgP auto + auto (no initiator). Both sides wait for each other, no channel forms, ports stay individual.
  • Protocol mismatch — Switch A uses LACP, Switch B uses PAgP. No common language, no channel, ports stay individual.
  • Mode on paired with a negotiation mode — Switch A mode on, Switch B mode active. The negotiation side never gets the handshake it needs, stays individual.
  • Partner not configured for EtherChannel — one side has channel-group configured, the other side has nothing. The configured side's ports stay individual.

Diagnosis: run show etherchannel summary and look for '(I)' on member ports. Then run show lacp neighbor or show pagp neighbor — if empty, the partner isn't sending negotiation frames. Check the partner's config: show running-config interface [port]. Verify both sides use the same protocol family and at least one is an initiator.

Fix: align the configurations. If it's a passive+passive / auto+auto situation, change one side to active/desirable. If it's a protocol mismatch, pick one protocol (usually LACP) and apply to both sides. If it's mode-on mixed with dynamic, either make both sides mode on or both sides dynamic with the same protocol.

I vs s distinction: 's' (lowercase) = suspended because of member config mismatch within the same switch. 'I' (uppercase) = individual because of negotiation failure across the link. Different problems, different fixes.

left.label: 's' (suspended)
items: Other members fine, this one out of uniform
Fix local config to match others

right.label: 'I' (individual)
items: Fix mode or protocol across the link

hack.memory: 'I' flag = 'Individual' = 'going it alone.' Negotiation across the link failed. Common causes: passive+passive, auto+auto, mode-on-paired-with-negotiation, LACP+PAgP mix. 'I' ≠ 's' — I is about cross-link negotiation, s is about local config match.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure Switch A LACP active, Switch B mode on. Observe member ports go to (I) — negotiation failure. Then align both sides to mode active — channel forms. Do the same experiment with LACP active on A and PAgP desirable on B — also produces (I) standalone.
hack.meta: Weight: medium. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 covers 'I' in the troubleshooting section. OCG Chapter 10. Exam may show 'I' in an output and ask for the cause — likely answer: mode or protocol mismatch across the link.

2.4.g.info:
Layer 3 EtherChannel (also called a routed port-channel) creates a routed logical interface with its own IP address — the port-channel operates at Layer 3, forwarding IP packets instead of L2 frames. Used for aggregated routed links between L3 devices: router-to-L3-switch, L3-switch-to-L3-switch, or router-to-router if the hardware supports EtherChannel.

Why use L3 EtherChannel: same reasons as L2 EtherChannel — aggregated bandwidth, link redundancy — but at the routing layer. Instead of a single routed link between two core switches, you bundle four links into a single routed interface with 4× the bandwidth. If a member fails, the routing table doesn't change (the Port-channel stays up), so OSPF/EIGRP/BGP don't reconverge for a single-link failure.

Key distinction from L2:

  • L2: member ports are switchports (switchport mode trunk or access); Port-channel has no IP; flag is SU (Switch layer, in Use).
  • L3: member ports are routed (no switchport); Port-channel has an IP address; flag is RU (Routed, in Use).

Configuration workflow:

! Step 1: convert members to routed ports BEFORE channel-group
interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 no switchport
 channel-group 1 mode active
!
! Step 2: put the IP on the Port-channel, not on members
interface Port-channel 1
 no switchport
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252
 no shutdown

The no switchport is critical — if members are still in switchport mode when you apply channel-group, the resulting channel will be L2, not L3. Order matters.

hack.memory: L3 EtherChannel = 'routed uplink, 4 cables.' Members are 'no switchport' (routed), IP goes on the Port-channel, flag is RU instead of SU. Used for L3↔L3 connections where you want aggregated routing bandwidth.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build an L3 channel between two L3 switches. Members: 'no switchport' + 'channel-group 1 mode active'. Port-channel: 'no switchport' + 'ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252'. Verify 'show etherchannel summary' shows RU. Ping from one Port-channel IP to the other.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 covers L3 EtherChannels. OCG Chapter 10. Less heavily tested than L2 but still shows up — know the 'no switchport' prereq and the RU flag.

2.4.g.info:
Every member port of a Layer 3 EtherChannel must be configured with no switchport BEFORE you assign it to the channel group. This converts the interface from a switchport (default on most Cisco switches) to a routed port — meaning it can carry IP traffic at L3 instead of L2 frames.

Why the order matters: when you apply channel-group 1 mode active to a switchport member, IOS creates (or uses) an L2 port-channel. If you then try to convert that port-channel to L3 with no switchport on the Port-channel interface, it can work but is error-prone. The cleanest workflow is: make the members routed first, then apply channel-group, then configure the IP on the Port-channel.

Configuration:

interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 no switchport         ! step 1: convert to routed port
 channel-group 1 mode active   ! step 2: add to channel group

After these two commands, IOS creates interface Port-channel 1 as a routed (L3) interface by default because its members are routed. You can then assign an IP:

interface Port-channel 1
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252

Common mistake: forgetting no switchport results in an L2 channel that accepts no IP. You'll see SU in summary output instead of RU and get an error when trying to apply ip address to the Port-channel. Fix: delete the channel-group on the members, apply no switchport, reapply channel-group. The IP commands then work.

Individual member IPs: even after no switchport, you do NOT configure IP addresses on the individual member ports. Only the Port-channel gets the IP. Members are just the physical plumbing under the logical L3 interface.

hack.memory: 'no switchport' = 'not a switchport, it's a routed port.' Required on every member BEFORE channel-group assignment for an L3 channel. Forget it and you'll get an L2 channel that refuses an IP. Order: no switchport → channel-group → then Port-channel + ip.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: try building an L3 channel both ways. (1) Correct order: 'no switchport' → 'channel-group 1 mode active' → 'interface Po1' → 'ip address ...'. Verify RU flag. (2) Forget 'no switchport': immediately do 'channel-group 1 mode active'. Try 'ip address' on Po1 — IOS rejects or creates L2 channel. Fix by removing channel-group, applying 'no switchport', reapplying. This contrast cements the ordering rule.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 demonstrates L3 channel creation with no switchport. OCG Chapter 10. The exam tests this as 'what's the prerequisite for an L3 EtherChannel?' → no switchport on the members.

2.4.g.info:
In a Layer 3 EtherChannel, the IP address goes on the Port-channel interface, never on the individual member ports. This is a hard rule — violating it breaks the channel.

Why only the Port-channel gets the IP: the Port-channel is the logical interface that the routing process sees. It's the interface the routing table reference, the one OSPF/EIGRP/BGP run over, the one you ping. The physical members are just parallel plumbing carrying the bits — they don't participate in L3 directly. If you tried to put IPs on individual members, each member would become its own L3 interface and the channel wouldn't aggregate properly.

Correct configuration:

! Members — no IP, just routed ports under the channel
interface range Gi0/1 - 2
 no switchport
 channel-group 1 mode active
!
! Port-channel — this is where the IP lives
interface Port-channel 1
 no switchport
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252

Verification:

Switch# show ip interface brief
