Interface              IP-Address      OK? Method Status
Gi0/1                  unassigned      YES unset  up up
Gi0/2                  unassigned      YES unset  up up
Port-channel1          10.0.0.1        YES manual up up

Notice the members show 'unassigned' — they don't have their own IPs. Only Port-channel1 has the 10.0.0.1 address. This is the correct state.

Routing protocols: when you add OSPF or EIGRP to this Port-channel interface, the protocol forms neighbor adjacencies over Po1, not over the individual members. Routing updates, neighbor hellos, and all L3 traffic use the Port-channel as their interface reference.

layers.label: Members Gi0/1, Gi0/2 → 'no switchport', no IP
Physical cables (carry the bits, no addressing)

hack.memory: IP on Port-channel, NEVER on members. The Port-channel is the 'phone number for the team'; the cables are just the wiring. Members show 'unassigned' in 'show ip interface brief' — that's correct for L3 EtherChannel.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure an L3 channel. Verify 'show ip interface brief' — Po1 has the IP, members show 'unassigned'. Try to add an IP to a member directly (e.g., 'interface Gi0/1' + 'ip address 10.0.0.5 /30') — IOS may accept it but it'll break the channel. Remove it. This proves the rule experientially.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Exam question: 'in a Layer 3 EtherChannel, where is the IP address configured?' → on the Port-channel interface, not the member interfaces.

2.4.g.info:
RU = Routed, in Use. The flag in show etherchannel summary that indicates a healthy Layer 3 EtherChannel. 'R' = Routed (as opposed to S = Switched/Layer 2). 'U' = in Use (as opposed to D = Down).

Compare SU vs RU:

  • SU — Switch layer (L2), in Use. Healthy L2 EtherChannel (trunk or access).
  • RU — Routed layer (L3), in Use. Healthy L3 EtherChannel with an IP.
  • SD — Switch layer, Down. L2 channel is broken.
  • RD — Routed layer, Down. L3 channel is broken.

Example healthy L3 output:

Group  Port-channel  Protocol    Ports
------+-------------+-----------+-----------------------
1      Po1(RU)       LACP        Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P)

Reading: port-channel 1, routed and in use, using LACP, both members bundled. This is the healthy state.

How to tell at a glance if an EtherChannel is L2 or L3: look at the first letter of the flag. S = L2, R = L3. This is the fastest way to verify you built what you intended — if you meant to build an L3 channel and you see SU, you forgot the no switchport prereq on the members.

Troubleshooting with the flag: if you see RD instead of RU, the L3 channel exists but is down. Check the member ports — are they (P), (D), or (s)? If all members are down, the channel can't be up. If members show (P) but channel is RD, check the Port-channel interface itself: show interface Port-channel 1 — look for administratively down, line protocol down, etc.

hack.memory: RU = Routed, in Use = healthy L3 channel. SU = Switched, in Use = healthy L2 channel. First letter tells you the layer (S or R), second letter tells you the state (U or D). Memorize: SU/RU = good, SD/RD = bad.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build one L2 channel and one L3 channel on the same switch. Run 'show etherchannel summary' — observe one as (SU) and the other as (RU). Shut down the L3 channel's Port-channel interface — verify it becomes (RD). Bring it back up — (RU) returns. This four-state demo (SU, SD, RU, RD) covers every possible channel-level flag.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 uses both flags. OCG Chapter 10 lists them. Exam-typical question: 'what does RU mean in show etherchannel summary?' → Routed (L3) EtherChannel, in use.

2.4.h.info:
SDVTS is a memory aid for the five settings that must be identical across all member ports of an EtherChannel. Any mismatch on any one of these causes the offending port to be suspended ('s' flag).

  • S — Speed. All members must negotiate (or be hard-set to) the same speed. You cannot mix 1 Gbps with 10 Gbps in the same channel. If one port auto-negotiates to 1 Gbps and another to 10 Gbps due to different connected gear, the slower port suspends.
  • D — Duplex. All members must be the same duplex (virtually always full-duplex on modern switches). Half-duplex vs full-duplex mismatch causes suspension.
  • V — VLAN (access VLAN). For access-mode EtherChannels, all members must be in the same access VLAN. Putting Gi0/1 in VLAN 10 and Gi0/2 in VLAN 20 suspends one of them.
  • T — Trunk configuration. For trunk-mode EtherChannels: same trunk mode, same trunk encapsulation (dot1q — ISL is legacy), same allowed VLAN list, same native VLAN. Changing any of these on one member vs another causes suspension.
  • S — STP settings. Same STP port cost, same STP port priority, same BPDU filter/guard settings. Less commonly tripped than VLAN/trunk mismatches but can still cause suspension.

Why these five: they define how the port behaves at L1, L2, and during STP convergence. If any one differs, the channel can't treat all members uniformly — the switch suspends the odd one out rather than risk incorrect frame forwarding.

Troubleshooting heuristic: when a port is suspended, check SDVTS in that order. Speed and duplex are usually obvious from show interfaces. VLAN and trunk config are in show run interface or show interfaces switchport. STP settings in show spanning-tree interface.

items: T — Trunk config: mode, encap, allowed, native (same)

hack.memory: SDVTS = 'Soldiers Don't Violate The Standard.' Speed, Duplex, VLAN, Trunk config, STP. Any mismatch = 's' flag. When troubleshooting, check SDVTS in order — VLAN and Trunk config are the most common offenders.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build a 4-port EtherChannel. Intentionally break each of the five SDVTS settings one at a time: (1) hard-set one member to 100 Mbps, (2) force one to half-duplex, (3) change one's access VLAN, (4) change one's trunk allowed list, (5) change one's STP cost. Each produces a suspended port. Fix each and verify re-bundling.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 labs SDVTS mismatches. OCG Chapter 10 lists the match requirements. Exam troubleshooting simlets present suspended-port output and ask for the cause — answer: SDVTS mismatch. This is heavily tested.

2.4.h.info:
Recovering a suspended port is painless: fix the configuration mismatch, and the port automatically re-bundles into the channel. No shutdown/no shutdown is required, no clearing the channel, no rebooting — just correct the config and the switch picks up the port again within a few seconds.

Recovery workflow:

  1. Identify the mismatch: run show etherchannel summary to see the (s) flag, then compare configs: show run interface [suspended-port] vs show run interface [bundled-port].
  2. Fix the mismatch: edit the suspended port's config to match the others, OR remove the member-level override and let the Port-channel push the correct config down.
  3. Watch it rebundle: within a few seconds, show etherchannel summary shows the port back at (P).

Example:

! Before: Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(s) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

Switch# show run interface Gi0/2
interface Gi0/2
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20    ! wrong — others have 10,20,30
 channel-group 1 mode active

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/2
Switch(config-if)# switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30

! After (seconds later): Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

Why no shut/no-shut is needed: the port's physical link never went down — it was just administratively suspended from the bundle. IOS continuously monitors member configs. As soon as the mismatch is resolved, the switch re-admits the port to the channel.

Exception: very rare cases where the switch doesn't automatically re-bundle (usually an IOS bug or a specific hardware quirk). In that case, a manual shutdown/no shutdown on the offending port forces re-negotiation. But this is uncommon — usually the auto-recovery just works.

hack.memory: Suspended port recovery = 'fix the config, walk away.' IOS auto-rebundles within seconds. No shut/no-shut, no reset needed. If it doesn't rebundle, then (and only then) try shut/no-shut.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: trigger a suspended port (VLAN mismatch). Note the (s) flag. Fix the VLAN. DO NOT issue shut/no-shut — just wait a few seconds and run 'show etherchannel summary' again. Verify the port is back at (P). This proves the auto-recovery behavior and prevents unnecessary disruption in real networks.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Exam-likely: 'what command is needed to recover a suspended EtherChannel member after fixing the config?' → none; it re-bundles automatically.

2.4.h.info:
Best-practice configuration order for a new EtherChannel:

  1. Plan the settings: decide L2 or L3, trunk vs access, VLANs allowed, native VLAN, IP (for L3), LACP vs PAgP vs static, mode (active/passive/desirable/auto/on).
  2. (L3 only) Prep members with no switchport: interface range Gi0/1 - 2 + no switchport. Skip this step for L2 channels.
  3. Assign members to the channel group: channel-group N mode [mode] on the range. This creates the Port-channel interface automatically.
  4. Configure the Port-channel interface: interface Port-channel N, then all L2 settings (trunk mode, allowed VLANs, native VLAN) or L3 settings (IP address). IOS pushes these down to members automatically.
  5. Verify: show etherchannel summary — look for SU or RU, all members at (P).

Why this order works: by configuring the Port-channel interface after the channel is formed, you guarantee all members receive the same config simultaneously via IOS's push-down mechanism. There's no window for drift.

Alternative order (also valid): some engineers configure the Port-channel interface first (which requires manually creating it with interface Port-channel N), then assign members. Both approaches work. The key invariant is: never configure trunk/VLAN/IP settings on individual members. Always use the Port-channel as the source of truth.

Anti-pattern to avoid: configuring each member individually with trunk/VLAN settings before channel-group assignment. This works if you get every setting perfectly identical on every port, but it's brittle — one typo and you have a suspended port. Use the Port-channel interface instead.

nodes: 3. channel-group N mode X on members

hack.memory: Best-practice order: plan → (L3 prep) → channel-group on members → configure on Port-channel → verify. Configuring on the Port-channel is the magic — IOS pushes settings down to all members at once. Prevents drift and suspended ports.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build an EtherChannel following the best-practice order exactly. Note how clean it is. Now build a second EtherChannel using the anti-pattern — configure each member individually first, then channel-group. Introduce one small typo (e.g., one member has allowed vlan 10,20 instead of 10,20,30). Watch that member get suspended. This contrast cements why the Port-channel-first approach is safer.
hack.meta: Weight: medium. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 shows the recommended workflow. OCG Chapter 10 endorses it. Exam asks 'what's the best practice for EtherChannel config?' → configure on the Port-channel interface, not on individual members.

2.4.i.info:
SU and RU are the two healthy channel-level flags in show etherchannel summary output.

  • SU = Switch (L2) + Use (active) = healthy Layer 2 EtherChannel carrying frames based on MAC/VLAN.
  • RU = Routed (L3) + Use (active) = healthy Layer 3 EtherChannel carrying IP packets via its Port-channel IP address.

The first letter (S or R) tells you the layer; the second letter (U) tells you the state. Seeing either SU or RU paired with all members at (P) means the channel is fully operational.

Example SU (L2 trunk):

Group  Port-channel  Protocol    Ports
1      Po1(SU)       LACP        Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

Example RU (L3 routed):

Group  Port-channel  Protocol    Ports
1      Po1(RU)       LACP        Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P)

How to use these flags in troubleshooting:

  • If you configured an L2 channel and see SU, you're good.
  • If you configured an L2 channel and see RU, something's odd — the Port-channel is routed when you expected switched. Check for an accidental no switchport somewhere.
  • If you configured an L3 channel and see SU instead of RU, you forgot no switchport on the members. Fix: tear down, add no switchport, rebuild.
  • If you see SD or RD (Down), the channel is broken. Check member ports and the Port-channel interface state.

hack.memory: SU = Switch+Up (L2 healthy). RU = Routed+Up (L3 healthy). First letter = layer, second letter = state. If you built L2 and see SU, celebrate. If you built L3 and see RU, celebrate. Anything else = investigate.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build one L2 and one L3 EtherChannel on the same switch. Run 'show etherchannel summary' — see SU on one, RU on the other. This is the quickest way to lock in the flag mapping.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 uses these flags constantly. OCG Chapter 10 documents them. Exam simlets test the flag meaning directly: 'what does SU mean?' → Layer 2, in Use.

2.4.i.info:
SD and RD are the unhealthy channel-level flags — they indicate the EtherChannel itself is in a Down state.

  • SD = Switch (L2) + Down = Layer 2 EtherChannel is not operational. Members may be up individually, but the channel as a whole is not forwarding.
  • RD = Routed (L3) + Down = Layer 3 EtherChannel is not operational. The Port-channel's IP interface is unreachable.

Common causes:

  • All member ports are down. If every member shows (D) — physical link failures, shutdowns, or suspensions — the Port-channel itself goes down. Fix the members.
  • Port-channel administratively shutdown. Someone ran shutdown on the Port-channel interface. Fix: no shutdown on interface Port-channel N.
  • Severe misconfiguration where no member can bundle. For example, protocol mismatch on every link would leave no member as (P), causing the channel to stay down.

Diagnosis:

  1. Check member states: run show etherchannel summary and look at the member flags. If all are (D) or (s) or (I), that explains the channel being down.
  2. Check the Port-channel interface: show interface Port-channel N. Look for 'administratively down, line protocol is down' (means shutdown is configured) or 'down, line protocol is down' (means all members failed).
  3. Check cabling and physical state: show interface Gi0/1 on each member. Any 'notconnect' or 'disabled' states?

Recovery: fix the root cause. Usually this means bringing at least one member to (P) state. If the Port-channel itself is shutdown, no shutdown it.

hack.memory: SD = Switched + Down = broken L2 channel. RD = Routed + Down = broken L3 channel. 'D' is never good. Usually means all members failed — fix the members and the channel returns to SU/RU.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build a 2-port EtherChannel, verify SU. Shut down both members ('shutdown' on interface range). Watch the channel go from SU to SD. Bring one member back up — does the channel return to SU? Yes, one (P) member is enough to keep the channel up. This tests the 'at least one bundled member keeps channel up' rule.
hack.meta: Weight: medium. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 covers SD/RD scenarios. OCG Chapter 10. Exam may show SD and ask 'what's the state of this channel?' → Layer 2, down. Or 'what could cause this?' → all members failed.

2.4.i.info:
The 'P' flag (uppercase P, for Port-channel member) means a member port is actively bundled into the Port-channel and forwarding traffic. This is the healthy per-port state — when all your members show (P), the channel is operating as designed.

What (P) indicates:

  • The port is physically up (link light green, cable good).
  • The port's configuration matches the other members (no SDVTS mismatch — so no 's' suspension).
  • The negotiation protocol (LACP or PAgP) completed successfully, or the mode is 'on' and both sides are on.
  • The port is actively carrying a share of the channel's traffic based on the load-balancing hash.

A healthy channel looks like:

Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

All four members bundled, channel in Use at L2. This is the baseline you should see every time you build an EtherChannel correctly.

Transitions:

  • (P) → (D): physical link failed (cable pulled, far end shut, etc.).
  • (P) → (s): member config was changed to something different from the others (VLAN, trunk, speed, duplex, or STP mismatch).
  • (P) → (I): negotiation failed (someone changed the mode on the partner side). More common on newly configured channels than on running ones.
  • (P) → (H): LACP decided to move this port to hot-standby (only happens when more than 8 ports are configured and a higher-priority port is present). Very rare on CCNA-scale labs.

transitions: (P) is the goal. Other states require action.

hack.memory: (P) = 'Perfect.' Bundled, forwarding, happy. When all members show (P), you're done. Any non-P flag requires investigation: s = config fix, I = negotiation fix, D = physical fix, H = standby (usually OK).
hack.practice: Every time you configure an EtherChannel in Packet Tracer, the first thing to verify is that every member shows (P). That single check catches 90% of EtherChannel issues.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 verifies (P) after every config. OCG Chapter 10. Exam-typical: an output is shown and you must identify which ports are 'bundled and active' → the ones with (P).

2.4.i.info:
The 's' flag (lowercase) marks a member port suspended from the channel due to a local configuration mismatch with other members. The port is physically up and would otherwise be healthy, but the switch refuses to bundle it because one or more of its settings differ from the rest of the channel.

Typical causes (the SDVTS list):

  • Different speed or duplex than other members.
  • Different access VLAN (for L2 access channels).
  • Different trunk allowed VLANs, native VLAN, or trunk encapsulation.
  • Different STP port cost or priority.

Example output:

Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P) Gi0/3(s) Gi0/4(P)

Gi0/3 is suspended. Channel is still up (SU) because the other three members are fine, but Gi0/3 isn't carrying any traffic. You've lost 25% of the channel's capacity until you fix the mismatch.

Diagnosis commands:

  • show running-config interface Gi0/3 vs show running-config interface Gi0/1 — compare line by line.
  • show interfaces Gi0/3 switchport — verify access VLAN, trunk mode, allowed list, native VLAN.
  • show interfaces Gi0/3 — verify speed and duplex.
  • show spanning-tree interface Gi0/3 — verify STP settings.

Fix: align Gi0/3's configuration with the others, or delete the member-level override and let the Port-channel push the correct config back down. The port automatically rebundles within seconds.

hack.memory: 's' = suspended = 'benched for being out of uniform.' Always caused by SDVTS mismatch on that port vs others. Fix with show-run-compare. Auto-rebundles once config is aligned.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: build a 4-port channel, all (P). Change one member's access VLAN to something different. Watch it go (s). Fix. Watch it return to (P). Repeat with 3 other SDVTS variables. This is the single most tested troubleshooting skill in EtherChannel.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 labs 's' extensively. OCG Chapter 10. Exam simlets show 's' in output and ask for cause — answer: SDVTS mismatch on that port.

2.4.i.info:
The 'I' flag (uppercase I, for Individual / stand-alone) means a member port is not bundled into the Port-channel — it's operating independently. This almost always indicates a negotiation failure between the local switch and the remote partner, not a local config mismatch.

Common causes:

  • Mode mismatch that breaks negotiation: LACP passive + passive (no initiator), or PAgP auto + auto (no initiator). Both ports sit waiting, no channel forms, they stay (I).
  • Protocol mismatch: Switch A LACP, Switch B PAgP. No common language. Both sides sit at (I).
  • Mode on paired with a negotiation mode: mode on doesn't send negotiation frames; the negotiation side waits for handshake that never comes; stays (I).
  • Remote side not configured for EtherChannel at all: local side has channel-group + mode, remote side has plain switchport configs with no channel-group. Local stays (I).

Example output:

Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(I) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P)

Wait — this is unusual because typically if negotiation fails, it fails on the whole bundle, not a single port. But if the remote side has mixed configs (some ports in the channel, some not), you can get individual (I)s. The more typical presentation is:

Po1(SD) LACP Gi0/1(I) Gi0/2(I) — channel entirely failed

Diagnosis: run show lacp neighbor or show pagp neighbor. If empty, no negotiation is happening. Check the remote side's config: show running-config interface [port]. Verify the same protocol and at least one initiator.

Fix: align modes and protocols across the link. Common fixes: set at least one side to active/desirable, ensure both sides use the same protocol family (both LACP or both PAgP).

hack.memory: 'I' = Individual = 'going solo.' Negotiation across the link failed. Main causes: passive+passive, auto+auto, mode-on-paired-with-negotiation, LACP+PAgP mix, or the remote side isn't configured. 's' is local; 'I' is cross-link.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: configure Switch A LACP active, Switch B LACP passive — channel forms ((P) on members). Now change Switch A to LACP passive (both sides passive) — watch members go to (I). Change A back to active — back to (P). Then try mode on vs mode active across the link — also (I).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23. OCG Chapter 10. Exam-typical: output shows (I), question asks cause — answer: negotiation mismatch (mode or protocol) across the link.

2.4.i.info:
The 'H' flag marks a member port as hot-standby — configured for the channel, negotiating LACP successfully, but not actively forwarding. It's on the bench, ready to instantly replace an active member if one fails. 'H' is an LACP-only flag — PAgP doesn't support hot-standby.

When you see 'H': only when more than 8 ports are configured in a single LACP channel group. LACP allows up to 16 configured ports, but only 8 can be active simultaneously. The other 8 sit as (H) waiting.

How LACP picks active vs standby: each port has an LACP port-priority (default 32768, configurable 0-65535, lower is better). LACP ranks all configured ports by priority and activates the 8 with the best priorities. The remaining 8 stay hot-standby. If you want a specific port to always be active, set its priority lower: lacp port-priority 100 on the interface.

Failover behavior: when an active (P) member fails (link down, shutdown, physical removal), the next-best (H) port is immediately promoted to (P) — no waiting for new negotiation because hot-standby ports were already negotiated with the partner and ready to forward. This gives sub-second failover at the member-port level.

Example output (hypothetical 10-port channel):

Po1(SU) LACP Gi0/1(P) Gi0/2(P) Gi0/3(P) Gi0/4(P) Gi0/5(P) Gi0/6(P) Gi0/7(P) Gi0/8(P) Gi0/9(H) Gi0/10(H)

8 active, 2 hot-standby. If Gi0/1 fails, Gi0/9 or Gi0/10 instantly becomes (P).

CCNA-scale note: most Packet Tracer labs and real-world access-layer channels use 2-4 members, well under the 8-active limit, so you won't see (H) in practice often. But the exam expects you to know what 'H' means.

transitions: Starting state sorts by LACP port-priority
Active member failure triggers standby promotion

hack.memory: 'H' = Hot-standby = 'on the bench, ready to sub in.' LACP-only. Only appears when more than 8 ports are configured. Lower port-priority = better chance of being active. Failover from (P)→(H)→(P) is near-instant.
hack.practice: Conceptual more than practical — Packet Tracer switches rarely have enough same-speed ports to lab 9+ members. Instead, memorize: 16 configured max, 8 active max, extras are (H), failover is automatic and fast.
hack.meta: Weight: medium. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 23 mentions hot-standby briefly. OCG Chapter 10 covers it. Exam-typical: 'what does H mean in show etherchannel summary?' → hot-standby, LACP-only, activates if an active member fails.
